schools.summary: The census the providential stage for the birth — the world at peace under one ruler (Bede), a 'census of souls' (Ambrose), the elect written in eternity (Gregory); Christ born when Judah's sceptre had failed (Genesis 49:10).

Henry: the 'proper time' — the fourth monarchy at its height (Daniel 2:44), Judea tributary, universal peace (Janus shut); Providence overrules Augustus's pride to bring Mary to Bethlehem (Micah 5:2).
Cyril of Alexandria: the timing fulfils Genesis 49:10; the two natures united without confusion in one Son, the Virgin truly Mother of God.

The Quirinius/Cyrenius dating crux: Luke distinguishes this census from the later one (Acts 5:37); resolved variously, but the latest scholarship (Ramsay, inscriptions) vindicates Luke as accurate — Quirinius was twice in Syria; Justin appeals to Roman registers.

debates.question: How is the dating to Quirinius reconciled with Jesus' birth under Herod?

sides.position: 'The first census Quirinius (the later governor) made' — distinguishing it from Acts 5:37
'Before Quirinius was governor' — taking protos as 'before' (John 1:30)
'The first under Quirinius's [earlier] governorship' — vindicated by inscriptions (Quirinius twice in Syria)

themes: when Cyrenius was governor of Syria

schools.summary: The Roman census required registration in the ancestral city — confirmed by Egyptian papyri ('enrolment by household,' Robertson) and possibly modified for Jewish feeling (Ellicott); 'a special note of accuracy' and the means of bringing Joseph and Mary to Bethlehem (Micah 5:2).

themes: every one to his own city

schools.summary: Joseph of David's house goes to Bethlehem per the census, fulfilling Micah 5:2; the two lines of descent may meet in him (Ellicott); Mary too of David's line as the throne-promise requires (Lightfoot).
Cyril of Jerusalem: 'wife' of a betrothed (like Rachel of Jacob before the wedding) does not imply Joseph was the father — defending the virgin birth.

themes: of the house and lineage of David

schools.summary: Mary, 'espoused' (truly married, Robertson) and 'great with child,' accompanies Joseph — as herself of David's line (her register in Bethlehem too) or for protection (Ellicott); God so orders it to fulfil Micah 5:2 (Clarke).
The virgin birth above nature — painless, immaculate, joy replacing Eve's sorrow (Gregory of Nyssa, Ambrose); Bethlehem ('house of bread') the fitting birthplace of the Bread of Life (Gregory, Bede).
The days fulfilled while they were at Bethlehem — God's providence timing the birth to the prophesied place, though Mary may not have known the prophecy required it (Wesley).
The manger and swaddling clothes the chosen lowliness of God — He becomes poor that we might be rich, wrapped in linen that we might receive the best robe (Chrysostom, Bede, Ambrose).
Calvin: the poverty and the manger are Christ's self-emptying (Philippians 2:7) — refused a lodging among men that heaven might be opened to us as our eternal country.
The manger a crib in a stall (cave or khan), no room in the kataluma because of the census crowds (Robertson); the birth shows poverty yet 'no dishonour to be poor' (Barnes).

debates.question: Does 'firstborn' imply Mary had other children?

sides.position: No — 'firstborn' means one before whom there is none, not necessarily with others following
Yes — it naturally implies later children (the brothers and sisters of Jesus)

schools.summary: The announcement comes to lowly shepherds (simple men like the patriarchs), not the scribes (Chrysostom); the night fitting, as a light arose in darkness (Bede); the shepherds figure the Church's vigilant teachers (Gregory, Bede, Origen).
Henry: the Magi (Gentiles) got a star, the shepherds (Jews) an angel — each in their own language; honour to lowly callings, as Moses and David were called from keeping sheep.
Bethlehem's pastures (where David kept sheep) and the season of field-watching by night (Gill, Lightfoot).
'The glory of the Lord' = the Shekinah, the cloud of divine brightness once granted to patriarchs and priests, now to shepherds (Ellicott); a great luminous appearance like Paul saw (Barnes).
Calvin: the glory was the outward sign by which God testified the message came from him, that the shepherds might receive it as from God's mouth; the fear humbles them to reverence.

themes: the glory of the Lord shone

schools.summary: 'Fear not' the normal accompaniment of angelic appearances (Ellicott); 'good tidings' the verb behind 'gospel' (Robertson); 'all the people' pointing first to Israel.

Calvin: solid joy begins in peace with God through Christ; the joy, first offered to Israel, now reaches all mankind, the wall of partition thrown down (Ephesians 2:14); Clarke: the good news is to men, not angels.

Three titles: Saviour (his work), Christ (anointed prophet-priest-king, Clarke), the Lord (Jehovah); 'Christ the Lord' unique in the NT (Robertson); the emperor's title 'Saviour' belongs truly to Christ.
Calvin: 'unto you' is emphatic — the gospel profits only as each applies the Saviour to himself (Isaiah 9:6); the angel recalls the Law and Prophets, the long-promised Redeemer.

themes: unto you is born a Saviour

schools.summary: The sign — the babe in swaddling clothes in a manger — became a sign by agreement with the prediction (Ellicott); Christ comes to reign by humility, the mark of the Son of God (Clarke).
Calvin: the angel forewarns of the mean appearance lest it deter faith, as God daily holds out Christ in the lowly elements of word and sacrament — no mean sign should hide his glory.

The heavenly host the King's army, vanquishing the powers of the air, rejoicing that redemption completes their number (Bede, Gregory); the angels of old judgment now sing thanksgiving at God's coming to men (Chrysostom).
'Heavenly host' a military term — the angels as the armies of the great King (Ellicott, Robertson); 'Here the army announces peace' (Bengel).
Calvin: the whole host bears witness to the Son of God, that we might join their choir — unbelief disturbing the harmony of heaven and earth is abominable.

themes: a multitude of the heavenly host

schools.summary: The anthem: glory to God for redemption, peace on earth; Glory/Peace and highest/earth and God/men correspond (Robertson); the better text reads 'peace among men of good will' (genitive) — peace among the objects of God's good pleasure (Ellicott).
Calvin: 'peace' is not outward concord but reconciliation with God (opposed to his wrath and the dread of death), flowing from his free good-will and mercy as its source.

debates.question: Is the last clause 'good will toward men' (nominative, a third member) or 'among men of good will' (genitive)?

sides.position: Genitive — 'peace among men of [God's] good pleasure'
Nominative — 'good will toward men' as a third clause / the subject of the whole

themes: glory to God in the highest

schools.summary: The shepherds seek 'the Word made flesh,' not merely a child (Bede); a real example of faith — simplicity, not pride; none seeks Christ indolently (Ambrose); they find Mary, Joseph, and the Saviour (Origen).
Calvin: their prompt obedience a model — the word must strike root and bear fruit at once; believers exhort one another; the messenger's word carries God's own authority.
'This thing' (rhema) a Hebraism — 'something said' used as 'something done,' suggesting Luke translated from Aramaic (Ellicott, Robertson).

themes: see this thing the Lord hath made known

schools.summary: The shepherds came with haste, searching and finding (Robertson); pious men seeking the Saviour without delay, a pattern for all who may always find him by faith (Barnes).
Calvin: the mean sight (a King in a manger) might have repelled, yet their faith, fixed on God's glory and word, rose above all that seemed despicable — a rebuke to our faith hindered by trifles.

themes: found Mary, Joseph, and the babe

schools.summary: Having seen the child, the shepherds publish the saying told them — becoming the first heralds of the gospel, their testimony the fruit of faith confirmed by sight (Gill, Calvin).
The shepherds figure the Church's teachers, ordained to explain what they have learned; having seen, they proclaim with simple, truthful eloquence (Bede, Gloss).

All who heard the shepherds wondered — the natural response to the marvellous tidings, attested by humble men who could not have invented them (Gill, Gloss); yet wonder is not yet faith (contrast Mary, v.19).

Mary weighs and compares each circumstance, treasuring it in faith (Barnes, Clarke); the intimate touch marks her as Luke's source — the narrative traceable to the Virgin herself (Ellicott, Robertson).

Calvin: her diligence laid up the treasure to be published at the proper time, and is an example for all the godly — to make contemplating God's faith-building works the chief object of life ('pondered' = collecting the events into one body).

The shepherds return to their flocks glorifying God, finding all as told — a pattern for ministers who, comparing prophecy with fact, return praising (Clarke); ordinary duty resumed but transformed by praise.

Calvin: their zeal (rising from the manger to heaven) rebukes our ingratitude — how much more should Christ's death and resurrection move us; the angelic word the rule by which they measured all, that God's truth might shine.

Christ circumcised to prove the reality of his flesh (against docetism, Epiphanius), to teach obedience and take compassion on those under the law (Bede), and as a figure of baptism now ceasing (Athanasius, Cyril); the name Jesus of heavenly origin (Origen).
Henry: Christ 'made under the law' (Galatians 4:4) — his obedience unto blood, owning himself Abraham's seed and a surety for our sins; the rite honours infant dedication (favouring infant baptism).
Circumcised on the eighth day per the law (Gill, Lightfoot); named Jesus as the angel directed before conception (1:31).

Mary, though exempt (virgin birth), submits voluntarily to the law, as Christ did, that we might be loosed from it (Bede); Christ presented for our sakes, that we might present ourselves (Athanasius); the doves figure the saints' virtues.
Calvin: the purification law proves original sin (the child born unclean) and its remedy in grace; Christ, the fountain of purity, took our uncleanness to wash away our pollution (Galatians 3:13).

'Their purification' (the older text) — Mary and the child, or Mary and Joseph (Robertson); the law witnesses to the taint of every child (Ellicott); the firstborn presented (Exodus 13:2) and redeemed (Numbers 18:15).

sides.position: 'Their' (the older reading) — Mary and the child, or Mary and Joseph

themes: to present him to the Lord

schools.summary: Luke cites the law of the firstborn (Exodus 13:2); the law did not require bringing the child to Jerusalem — the purification concerned the mother, the presentation the son (Robertson); Jesus 'made under the law' (Galatians 4:4).
Christ alone truly 'opened the virgin's womb' by a miraculous birth, retaining the seal of chastity (Gregory of Nyssa, Ambrose); alone 'holy' by nature, not mere dedication (1:35).
Calvin: the law instituted in memory of the sparing of Israel's firstborn (Exodus 12:29); the holy family's obedience 'as it is written' teaches us to worship only as God's Word requires.

themes: every male that openeth the womb

schools.summary: The offering of the poor — two doves, the substitute for a lamb (Leviticus 12:8) — proves the holy family's poverty; yet poverty is no dishonour where God places us (Barnes, Clarke).
Calvin: the Magi's gold was a small gift of respect, not enough to make them rich; they gave as their means allowed; the law mercifully distinguished poor from rich in the kind of sacrifice.
The poverty is Christ's chosen condescension — the rich Lord became poor that we might be made rich (2 Corinthians 8:9, Bede).

Simeon just and devout, waiting for 'the consolation of Israel' — not worldly happiness but the passing from the law's shadow to the truth (Gregory of Nyssa); his taking the child the law yielding to gospel grace, the old man embracing the new life (Bede).
Simeon a just, devout man with the Spirit upon him, distinct from the rabbinic Simeons (Lightfoot); led to the temple to receive the Christ.
Cyril of Alexandria: Christ 'made under the law to redeem them under the law,' subject to his own decrees for the plan of salvation.

themes: waiting for the consolation of Israel

schools.summary: A divine oracular revelation (the verb used of oracles and God's commands), answering Simeon's yearnings (Robertson, Ellicott); 'not see death' a Hebraism (Psalm 89:48); 'the Lord's Christ' = the Anointed of Jehovah.

Clarke: the devout soul a fit habitation for the Spirit; 'they that seek shall find' — none earnestly seeking God's salvation dies without finding it; Barnes: the promise now given to all who see Christ by faith.

themes: it was revealed by the Holy Ghost
not see death before seeing the Lord's Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the Spirit, knowing the exact time, moved and directed Simeon to come (not bodily transported); Clarke: fulfilling Malachi 3:1 (the Lord coming to his temple) — those led by the Spirit to worship meet the Consolation of Israel.
Origen: Simeon held the Liberator of the captive, who alone could loose the chains of the body; Ambrose: embrace the Word with the arms of faith, then depart having seen Life.
Theophylact: in saying 'Lord' Simeon confesses the child to be God, Lord of life and death.

themes: took him up in his arms

schools.summary: Clarke: 'loosest him from life,' death unnamed — to one who has seen Christ death loses its sting and its name; Barnes: God never disappoints, fulfilling the improbable promise to an old man; Gill: the humility of 'thy servant.'

themes: now lettest thou thy servant depart

schools.summary: Gill: Christ named 'salvation' as its author and substance, in whom alone it is had; Simeon saw him with bodily eyes (which prophets desired) and with the eye of faith, the latter giving the peace.
Ellicott: the neuter 'that which works salvation,' echoing Isaiah 52:10 (LXX); Clarke: Christ is our salvation as our life, peace, hope.
Robertson/Ellicott: 'all the peoples,' not merely Jews — Luke's universality; Clarke: a feast for all nations (Isaiah 25:6).
Gill: salvation prepared in God's eternal decree, the OT promises, types, and sacrifices, now sent in the flesh.
Gill: 'Light' a Messianic name (Isaiah 42:6; 49:6), the Gentiles enjoying his light who were in darkness; Barnes: ignorance fleeing as night before the sun (Isaiah 60:3).
Barnes: the glory of Israel, among whom he was born and to whom salvation first offered — light to the dark-dwelling Gentiles, glory to the covenant people.

Bede: Joseph called 'father' by reputation (for Mary's honour); Augustine: by the bond of wedlock, closer than adoption, not carnal; Origen: for the genealogy's sake (the line through Joseph) — guarding the virgin birth.
The parents marvel anew at Simeon's testimony, the supernatural renewing the wonder, after the angel, the host, and the shepherds.

debates.question: In what sense is Joseph called Jesus's 'father'?

sides.position: By reputation, the marriage-bond, and the genealogy — not carnal paternity

schools.summary: Barnes: 'the fall' = ruin, stumbling over the lowly Messiah (Isaiah 8:14-15), the proud cast down; 'rising' = the humbled raised to a true righteousness; Christ the dividing touchstone, a sign spoken against.
Ambrose: grace extended to all, prophecy withheld from unbelievers not the righteous — the fall a matter of faith's rejection.

themes: the fall and rising of many

schools.summary: Gill/Barnes: the sword = Mary's maternal grief at Christ's sufferings, above all at the cross; 'thoughts revealed' = Christ exposing the secret hearts by their response to him.
Lightfoot/Epiphanius: a minority read the sword of Mary's martyrdom, though Scripture is silent.

debates.question: What is the 'sword' that pierces Mary's soul?

sides.position: Her grief at Christ's sufferings, chiefly at the cross
Her own martyrdom (a minority, Scripture silent)

themes: a sword shall pierce thy soul

schools.summary: Ambrose: every condition witnesses to Christ — aged man, wife, virgin, and now widow; Origen: Anna's long chastity and fasting raised her to the virtue fitting her for prophecy; her tribe named for credible distinction.

Gill: the 84 years her widowhood (so of great age); Barnes: her total age, spent in continual worship; Lightfoot: 'departed not' = constant at the stated services, dwelling near the temple for devotion — the pattern of holy widowhood (1 Timothy 5:5).

themes: a widow of fourscore and four
fastings and prayers night and day

schools.summary: Clarke: she came as Simeon sang, gave public thanks, and 'spake of him' to the devout who awaited redemption (Daniel's weeks fulfilled) — a redemption by atonement/ransom; one of the first publishers of the gospel; variant 'Israel'/'Jerusalem.'

Bede: Luke omits the flight to Egypt (told by Matthew), the evangelists each omitting what another relates; Augustine reconciles Joseph's settling in Galilee not Judea (fearing Archelaus); the Greek expositor: Luke describes before the descent to Egypt.

debates.question: How is Luke's direct return to Nazareth reconciled with Matthew's flight to Egypt?

sides.position: Luke omits the Egypt sojourn (told by Matthew); the Gospels are complementary

themes: performed all according to the law

schools.summary: Gill: a true body growing by stages, the soul's faculties opening gradually — yet his divine wisdom and the fulness in him as Mediator could not increase; the growth in his human nature.
Cyril: the Rich descended to our poverty, truly man, obedient to the law, sanctifying every stage of human development to make us rich.

debates.question: How does the all-wise Word 'grow' in wisdom?

sides.position: In his human nature only — his divine wisdom could not increase

themes: the grace of God upon him

schools.summary: Gill: the law bound the males to three annual feasts; women (Mary) went voluntarily, here out of devotion.
Henry: their constant, costly attendance despite poverty and distance a model — frequent the ordinances; they went though they had the Son of God at home, for the Lord loves the gates of Zion.
Cyril/Greek expositor: his wisdom appears at twelve, the age discernment matures, not exceeding his human growth's measure — preserving his true humanity.
Bede: the annual pilgrimage betokens Christ's humility as man; symbolic readings of twelve (the apostles, Ambrose; the perfection of times, Bede) as the start of his manifested glory.

themes: after the custom of the feast

schools.summary: Gill/Barnes: the seven (or eight) days of unleavened bread (Exodus 12:15; Leviticus 23:5-6), which the devout kept fully; Lightfoot on the rabbinic dispute over departure; the boy tarried unknown to his parents.

Barnes/Clarke: the great caravan of families, men and women (and children) in separate groups, each parent supposing the boy with the other — no negligence; they sought him among kinsfolk before realizing he was left behind.

Gill: not found in the caravan or among kin, they turned back; Clarke/Robertson: 'seeking diligently' (anazētountes), the search intensified by his absence — the proper earnestness in seeking the lost Christ.

Gill/Barnes: the three days reckoned (out, back, searching); Lightfoot on the temple courts and the doctors' custom of teaching; the boy sitting as a learner, hearing and questioning — not as a master, yet with rare discernment.

themes: in the midst of the doctors

schools.summary: Robertson: continued astonishment, 'understanding' = grasp comparing and combining, a holy thirst using a boy's way of learning; Ellicott: discernment in his questions too (cf. Josephus's boast); the doctors amazed beyond a clever child's precocity.

Robertson: 'struck out' — even the parents had not grasped his power; Barnes: 'thy father' by legal/reputed usage (the virgin birth not public); Clarke: they laid the blame on him though it was theirs.
Calvin: Mary's complaint not ambition but grief of three days; Ellicott on the continuous 'we were seeking thee.'

themes: why hast thou thus dealt with us

schools.summary: Barnes: a gentle reproof — they should have known the Son of God was safe; 'my Father's business'/'house' both showing his higher Father and the necessity ('I must') of his mission (John 9:4); 'my Father' set against Mary's 'thy father.'

debates.question: Should it read 'about my Father's business' or 'in my Father's house'?

sides.position: 'In my Father's house' (the temple) — why they should have sought him there

themes: how is it ye sought me

schools.summary: Barnes: they understood not — how slow to believe; Robertson: even Mary not equal to the dawning Messianic consciousness (God called Father, not Master); Ellicott answers the sceptic — childhood years dulled the impressions, no real inconsistency with the annunciation.

debates.question: Does the parents' incomprehension contradict the annunciation?

sides.position: No — years of ordinary childhood dulled the impressions; understanding grows by degrees

schools.summary: Origen: the Son of God subject to Joseph and Mary — the greater rightly subject to the lesser, a lesson in obedience; the Greek expositor: he preferred God to parents (the temple) yet honoured parents (Nazareth), in right order.
Bede: the teacher of virtue fulfils his duty to parents, doing among us what he would have done by us; Mary kept and pondered the sayings.

outliers.note: Marvels that the greater (Jesus) is subject to the lesser, drawing the lesson that the truly great may humbly submit.

schools.summary: Gill: increased in wisdom as man (his divine wisdom and mediatorial fulness could not increase), the soul's faculties opening gradually by the indwelling Deity and the unmeasured Spirit; Barnes: 'favour with God' = ever more visible, not previously lacking.
Barnes: parallel to Samuel (1 Samuel 2:26); the practical lesson for children who imitate the obedient, growing Jesus.

debates.question: How does the all-wise Christ 'increase in wisdom and favour'?

sides.position: In his human nature only; the divine wisdom unchangeable, the favour ever more visibly shown
